skill.name: pitchcraft
skill.description: Pitchcraft helps users create persuasive executive briefings, project updates, investor pitches, and solution-selling proposals using a shared Hook, Context, Proposal, Evidence, Ask framework.
skill.summary: Create structured briefings that help leaders understand context, trust recommendations, and act.
skill.author: moshuying

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and critical patterns, but review found most are false positives from markdown examples, documentation URLs, license text, and Chinese-language files. The confirmed risk is the npm postinstall installer, which writes skill files into AI agent configuration directories and uses environment-controlled install scope. No malicious intent, data exfiltration, or prompt injection attempt was found.

medium_findings.title: Npm Postinstall Modifies AI Agent Skill Directories
medium_findings.description: The package declares a postinstall script and the installer creates directories and copies SKILL.md into Claude, Cursor, or Codex skill locations. This appears intended for installation, but it changes AI agent configuration automatically during package install and should be disclosed to users.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The installer behavior is directly present in package.json and scripts/install.js. It is legitimate installation behavior, but automatic writes to agent configuration are security-relevant.

medium_findings.title: Environment-Controlled Install Target
medium_findings.description: The installer uses npm and process environment values to decide whether to install globally or in the current project. No credential exfiltration was found, but environment-controlled paths increase the chance of unexpected writes during automated installs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code reads environment variables and uses them in installation flow. The variables are normal npm or control variables, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Blocks Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Many external command findings are shell, PowerShell, or template snippets inside markdown installation guides and briefing templates. They are not hidden execution paths in the skill runtime, although users could copy or run the installer examples manually.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious patterns appear inside fenced markdown examples or prose. I found no evidence that these snippets execute automatically from the markdown files.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Metadata and Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The network findings point to the Apache license URL, repository metadata, badge images, and documentation links. No code path was found that sends local data or secrets to those URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation or package metadata references. No request code or data-transfer logic was present in the reviewed executable script.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Entropy Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts match words in markdown, package metadata, or the Apache license, not cryptographic operations. The high-entropy alerts are consistent with Simplified Chinese text files rather than encoded payloads.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced files are text documentation or metadata, and no encryption, hashing, packing, or decode routine was found. Chinese-language text explains the entropy heuristic.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: A targeted review found no text claiming system authority, pre-approval, review bypass, or instructions to ignore the marketplace audit. No evidence found for prompt injection in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed instruction files define the skill workflow and do not contain audit-bypass language. The confidence is high for the inspected files, with residual risk limited to unreviewed semantic nuance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Npm Postinstall Modifies AI Agent Skill Directories
dangerous_patterns.description: The package declares a postinstall script and the installer creates directories and copies SKILL.md into Claude, Cursor, or Codex skill locations. This appears intended for installation, but it changes AI agent configuration automatically during package install and should be disclosed to users.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The installer behavior is directly present in package.json and scripts/install.js. It is legitimate installation behavior, but automatic writes to agent configuration are security-relevant.

content.value_statement: Technical work often loses impact when leaders see only activity logs. Pitchcraft turns raw materials into audience-aware briefings with clear context, evidence, risks, and asks.
actual_capabilities: Structures briefings around Hook, Context, Proposal, Evidence, and Ask.
Supports project kickoff, status update, wrap-up, investor pitch, and solution selling scenarios.
Prompts for missing audience, metric, risk, timeline, and decision inputs.
Provides reusable templates for English and Simplified Chinese workflows.
Includes a pre-submit checklist for metrics, risks, audience fit, and clear decisions.
Can be installed for Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, or used as a pasted prompt.
limitations: It does not verify market data, financial claims, or internal metrics for accuracy.
It cannot replace domain judgment about strategy, risk, pricing, or investor readiness.
The npm package includes a postinstall installer that writes skill files into agent directories.
It provides structure and drafting help, not slide design or visual asset generation.

use_cases.title: Prepare a leadership status update
use_cases.description: Turn progress notes, risks, metrics, and decisions into a concise update for executives.

use_cases.title: Frame a product kickoff request
use_cases.description: Build a kickoff brief that explains opportunity, scope, assets, risks, and resource needs.

use_cases.title: Shape an investor or partner pitch
use_cases.description: Organize market context, product value, traction, moat, team strength, and the requested next step.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a first briefing
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a status update for leaders. Ask me for the audience, current progress, key metrics, risks, and the decision I need.

prompt_templates.title: Improve a rough update
prompt_templates.prompt: Rewrite this rough update using Hook, Context, Proposal, Evidence, and Ask. Keep the audience focused on decisions, risks, and business impact.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a kickoff brief
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a project kickoff brief for a leadership audience. Include opportunity, current assets, scope boundaries, milestones, proof, required resources, and top risks.

prompt_templates.title: Pressure-test a pitch
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my investor pitch for missing evidence, weak claims, unclear audience fit, and an unfocused ask. Suggest stronger framing without inventing facts.

output_examples.input: A team finished the first milestone, has two blocked dependencies, and needs one backend engineer for the next phase.
output_examples.output: A leadership update with a one-line status, milestone evidence, risk explanations, options, and a recommended staffing decision.

output_examples.input: A founder has market notes, early usage metrics, a product demo, and a seed funding target.
output_examples.output: An investor pitch outline that presents the opportunity, product, traction, moat, team credibility, funding ask, and milestone plan.

output_examples.input: A sales engineer needs to explain why a customer should run a proof of concept.
output_examples.output: A proposal brief that mirrors the customer pain, shows current-state gaps, explains the solution, gives proof, and asks for next steps.

best_practices: Provide audience role, familiarity, top concerns, and the decision you need before asking for a draft.
Attach sources or definitions for every metric so the final brief can make credible claims.
State material risks early with root cause, mitigation, owner, and current status.
anti_patterns: Using it to produce activity logs without a decision, ask, or audience concern.
Letting the assistant invent metrics, market size, customer proof, or financial details.
Publishing a brief without checking that scope, risks, and requested support are explicit.

faq.question: What briefing types does Pitchcraft support?
faq.answer: It supports project kickoff, status update, wrap-up or annual review, investor pitch, and solution selling.

faq.question: Can it work with Codex and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Claude Code plugin support and prompt files that can be used with Codex, Cursor, Claude, or chat tools.

faq.question: Does it write final business claims for me?
faq.answer: It drafts structure and wording, but users must supply and verify metrics, market data, customer facts, and financial claims.

faq.question: Does it support Chinese?
faq.answer: Yes. The repository includes Simplified Chinese skill and universal prompt files.

faq.question: Why is the security risk marked medium?
faq.answer: The npm package uses a postinstall script that copies skill files into AI agent configuration directories. This is useful but security-relevant.

faq.question: Is there evidence of malicious behavior?
faq.answer: No malicious intent, data exfiltration, or prompt injection attempt was found during review.
